jokes.text: Bust my hump all week. Stupid grass! Supposed to be the boy's job.
jokes.setup: Homer is doing yard work despite it being Bart's responsibility
jokes.punchline: Homer's grumbling about doing work that's literally assigned to someone else
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer laziness and blame-shifting. Good character integration but fairly standard complaint humor.

jokes.text: You heard the lady, Homer. So please mow quietly. Genius at work.
jokes.setup: Bart is supposedly working on a science project
jokes.punchline: Bart pompously calls himself a genius while avoiding yard work
jokes.explanation: Good Bart cockiness, and the 'genius at work' line has nice quotable quality. Well-integrated character moment.

jokes.text: One o'clock. Still just a potato.
jokes.setup: Bart's 'genius' science project is revealed
jokes.punchline: After hours of 'work,' his project is literally just a potato
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to the 'genius at work' setup. Perfect economy and deflation of Bart's pretensions. The deadpan delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Hi, Flanders. -Doing yard work? -Who told? Marge, beer me.
jokes.setup: Flanders asks if Homer is doing yard work
jokes.punchline: Homer's paranoid reaction as if yard work is a shameful secret, then immediately demands beer
jokes.explanation: Great Homer characterization - treating normal behavior like a conspiracy, then pivoting to beer. Good rapid-fire comedy.

jokes.text: There's nothing wrong with crabgrass. It just has a bad name. Everyone would love it if it was called 'elfgrass.'
jokes.setup: Flanders points out Homer's crabgrass problem
jokes.punchline: Homer's ridiculous logic that the problem is just marketing/naming
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons absurdist logic. Homer's complete misunderstanding of the problem is perfectly in character and genuinely clever wordplay.

jokes.text: Don't toy with me, woman.
jokes.setup: Marge offers fruit juice instead of beer
jokes.punchline: Homer's overly dramatic response to a minor beverage substitution
jokes.explanation: Homer treating a beverage offer like a betrayal. Good character moment with quotable delivery.

jokes.text: We've only been neighbors for one, two, three, four-- Eight years.
jokes.setup: Ned commenting on Homer's first visit to their house
jokes.punchline: The counting pause reveals they've been neighbors for 8 years despite never socializing
jokes.explanation: Good reveal of how disconnected the neighbors are, with nice timing on the count.

jokes.text: There's my little popcorn ball. Kissy, kissy.
jokes.setup: Ned greeting his wife
jokes.punchline: Overly cutesy pet names and baby talk
jokes.explanation: Establishes Ned's overly perfect family dynamic. The cringe factor works for character building.

jokes.text: Beggars can't be choosey.
jokes.setup: Ned offers premium Dutch beer from his personal tap
jokes.punchline: Homer treats this luxury as if he's doing Ned a favor by accepting
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer ingratitude. The irony is sharp - he's getting better treatment than he deserves but acts like he's being shortchanged.

jokes.text: Toodley doodley.
jokes.setup: Father-son bonding moment between Ned and Todd
jokes.punchline: Todd's nonsensical cute catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Memorable nonsense phrase that became a Todd Flanders signature. Works as character establishment.

jokes.text: Your wife's butt is higher than my wife's butt. You make me sick.
jokes.setup: Homer listing ways the Flanders family is better
jokes.punchline: The escalation reaches an absurd and inappropriate comparison
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that goes from reasonable complaints to deeply inappropriate. Homer's complete lack of filter makes this a standout moment.

jokes.text: One for the road.
jokes.setup: Homer storms out in anger after insulting Ned
jokes.punchline: But first grabs another beer on his way out
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment. Homer's priorities are crystal clear - even righteous anger doesn't override free beer. Excellent visual comedy.

jokes.text: Well, it was how he said it. -How did he say it? -He.... -Was he angry? -No. -Rude? -It wasn't how he said it either... but the message was loud and clear. Our family stinks.
jokes.setup: Marge asking what Ned said to upset Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer realizes he has no actual complaint but doubles down anyway
jokes.explanation: Great setup-payoff structure. Homer's inability to justify his anger while maintaining it anyway is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Don't backpedal. You were right. He's perfect. Perfect in every way.
jokes.setup: Marge trying to moderate her statement about Ned
jokes.punchline: Homer seizing on her words to fuel his victimhood complex
jokes.explanation: Homer's self-destructive spiral - refusing comfort to maintain his grievance.

jokes.text: I'm not perfect, like Ned Flanders.
jokes.setup: Homer walking around the block to cool down
jokes.punchline: Homer's self-pitying comparison to Ned
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's self-awareness combined with self-pity.

jokes.text: Probably stepped on a worm.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Lovejoy saying Ned is having a crisis
jokes.punchline: Lovejoy's dismissive assumption about what constitutes a crisis for Ned
jokes.explanation: Excellent characterization of both Ned's gentle nature and Lovejoy's cynicism. Great economy in the joke.

jokes.text: Oh, Matthew 19: 19, yeah, right.
jokes.setup: Ned citing a specific Bible verse
jokes.punchline: The reverend clearly doesn't know the verse and fakes familiarity
jokes.explanation: Great hypocrisy joke - the religious leader caught unprepared by actual scripture knowledge.

jokes.text: 'Bosom.' Wait, wait, there's more.
jokes.setup: Homer reading Ned's heartfelt apology letter
jokes.punchline: Homer fixating on the word 'bosom' like a child
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer immaturity. The contrast between Ned's sincere emotion and Homer's juvenile reaction works well.

jokes.text: Read the bosom part again, Dad.
jokes.setup: Homer was just giggling at 'bosom'
jokes.punchline: Bart encouraging his father's immaturity
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows father-son bonding through shared immaturity.

jokes.text: Too bad we don't live on a farm. Let's go.
jokes.setup: Family discussing what activity to do together
jokes.punchline: Homer's completely random farm comment followed by immediate departure
jokes.explanation: Homer's random thought process. Not particularly strong but fits his scattered thinking pattern.

jokes.text: But I got it in the middle.
jokes.setup: Homer playing mini golf
jokes.punchline: Homer's confusion about how mini golf works despite obvious failure
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer misunderstanding of basic concepts. Shows his competitive delusion.

jokes.text: D'oh! That was practice. / Play it where it lays.
jokes.setup: Homer clearly missing his shot badly
jokes.punchline: Claiming it was practice, then Ned's firm rule enforcement
jokes.explanation: Classic sports comedy - the cheating attempt followed by rule enforcement. Homer's 'D'oh!' is iconic.

jokes.text: Get bent.
jokes.setup: Todd cheerfully greeting Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart's immediate hostile response to friendliness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart rudeness. The contrast with Todd's sweetness makes it funnier.

jokes.text: It went in. -Good shot. -Luck.
jokes.setup: Homer makes a difficult mini golf shot after Ned's advice
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately dismisses his success to avoid giving Ned credit
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Homer's refusal to acknowledge help. Perfect pettiness.

jokes.text: Yeah? The fruit of my loins can beat the fruit of your loins any day.
jokes.setup: Discussion of the boys' mini golf abilities
jokes.punchline: Homer's overwrought biblical phrasing for a silly competition
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of competitive language. 'Fruit of my loins' for mini golf is perfectly absurd and quotable.

jokes.text: This is the only time I will ever say this. It is not okay to lose.
jokes.setup: Homer giving Bart a pep talk
jokes.punchline: Homer dramatically reversing his usual parenting philosophy for mini golf
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Homer's backwards priorities. The formal phrasing makes it more absurd.

jokes.text: what a violin is to the guy-- The violin guy.
jokes.setup: Homer making an analogy about putters
jokes.punchline: Homer can't remember 'violinist' despite having just used the word violin
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer brain malfunction. Shows his limited vocabulary in a very specific, funny way.

jokes.text: Give it a name. -What? -Give it a name. -Mr. Putter. -Wanna try a little harder? -Give it a girl's name. -Mom. -It's Charlene.
jokes.setup: Homer insisting Bart name his putter
jokes.punchline: The progression from generic to inappropriate (Mom) to Homer just deciding
jokes.explanation: Good escalation comedy. Bart suggesting 'Mom' is appropriately weird, and Homer's arbitrary decision is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Sometimes the only way to feel good is to make someone feel bad... and I'm tired of making people feel good.
jokes.setup: Marge questioning why they need to show up the Flanders
jokes.punchline: Homer's twisted philosophy about happiness through others' misery
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny philosophy that's both twisted and oddly relatable. Homer's honesty about his petty motivations is refreshing.

jokes.text: A blockaded bishop is of little value, but I think you mean a pawn.
jokes.setup: Bart asking about chess pieces that don't matter
jokes.punchline: Lisa's overly precise chess knowledge while still answering his real question
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment - showing off knowledge while still being helpful. Good contrast with Bart's simple question.

jokes.text: 'Anecdotes,' 'Eisenhower and,' 'Humor,' 'Japanese Obsession With'....
jokes.setup: Lisa looking up golf in the card catalog
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific subcategories, especially 'Japanese Obsession With'
jokes.explanation: Great background gag writing - the library catalog categories are hilariously specific and culturally observant.

jokes.text: Piece of cake.
jokes.setup: Lisa presents the ancient riddle of one hand clapping
jokes.punchline: Bart confidently dismisses a 3000-year-old philosophical puzzle
jokes.explanation: Great Bart arrogance meeting ancient wisdom. The confidence in his dismissal is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Absolutely. But, Bart, how can sound exist if there's no one there to hear it?
jokes.setup: Lisa poses the tree falling philosophical question
jokes.punchline: Bart's immediate confident answer followed by Lisa's confusion at his certainty
jokes.explanation: Good followup to the previous philosophical puzzle. Bart's practical thinking vs Lisa's intellectual approach.

jokes.text: You've actually found a practical use for geometry!
jokes.setup: Lisa teaching Bart golf through geometric principles
jokes.punchline: Her amazement that math has real-world application
jokes.explanation: Relatable academic humor - the surprise when theoretical knowledge proves useful in real life.

jokes.text: Get down before the neighbors see-- / Simpson!
jokes.setup: Homer doing embarrassing training routine in yard
jokes.punchline: Marge's mortification interrupted by Ned's appearance
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy setup - embarrassing behavior witnessed by worst possible person.

jokes.text: 'May the best man win.' The loser's mating call.
jokes.setup: Ned's sportsmanlike comment
jokes.punchline: Homer's cynical reinterpretation as admission of defeat
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cynical observation - reframing politeness as psychological weakness. Very quotable.

jokes.text: I'm not a betting man. -I'm a chicken, am I? -Right.
jokes.setup: Ned claiming he's not a betting man
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate insult and Ned's unexpected agreement
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection. Ned's honest self-assessment catches Homer off guard and us too.

jokes.text: You have yourself a bet, you jackaninny!
jokes.setup: Ned getting worked up about the bet
jokes.punchline: Ned's attempt at a harsh insult results in a made-up cute word
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment. Ned trying to be mean but his nature makes him invent adorable fake insults. 'Jackaninny' is perfect.

jokes.text: 'Loser' is such a harsh word. How about 'the boy who doesn't win'?
jokes.setup: Reading back the terms of their bet
jokes.punchline: Ned's overly gentle euphemism for losing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned politeness. The euphemism is both funny and completely in character for his gentle nature.

jokes.text: That crazy Marmaduke. / Eighth hole? / Octopus, third tentacle.
jokes.setup: Pre-game mental preparation routine
jokes.punchline: The contrast between Bart's casual comic reading and Lisa's intense coaching
jokes.explanation: Great character contrast - Bart's casual attitude vs Lisa's systematic approach. 'Crazy Marmaduke' is perfect throwaway.

jokes.text: Steak will make him logy. -What won't make him logy? -Oatmeal. -Oatmeal?
jokes.setup: Lisa explaining Bart's dietary needs
jokes.punchline: Homer's incredulous reaction to the mundane suggestion
jokes.explanation: Homer's disbelief at normal healthy food recommendations. Shows his disconnect from proper nutrition.

jokes.text: News flash, Lisa. Bart is not a horse. Eat your steak, boy.
jokes.setup: Lisa's racehorse analogy for oatmeal
jokes.punchline: Homer's literal-minded dismissal and return to meat
jokes.explanation: Homer's practical thinking overriding Lisa's logic. 'Bart is not a horse' is a good quotable line.

jokes.text: Having never received encouragement, I'm not sure how it should sound. But here goes. I believe in you.
jokes.setup: Marge trying to encourage Bart
jokes.punchline: Marge's honest admission about the family's lack of encouragement
jokes.explanation: Brilliant self-aware moment. Marge acknowledging the family's dysfunction while trying to break the pattern is both funny and touching.

jokes.text: I already prayed, and we can't both win.
jokes.setup: Ned trying to pray before the tournament
jokes.punchline: Bart's logical assessment of prayer's limitations
jokes.explanation: Great kid logic about prayer. Bart's practical approach to theology is both funny and oddly wise.

jokes.text: Afraid so, infinity plus one.
jokes.setup: Competitive banter between Homer and Ned
jokes.punchline: Homer using playground argument tactics in adult context
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer childishness. Using kid logic in adult competition is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Mercy's for the weak, Todd.
jokes.setup: Competitive mini golf tournament
jokes.punchline: Homer giving harsh advice to the wrong kid
jokes.explanation: Homer's inappropriate coaching directed at the opponent's child. Dark humor with good quotability.

jokes.text: Remember what Vince Lombardi said! 'Lose, and you're out of the family.'
jokes.setup: Homer invoking famous football coach wisdom
jokes.punchline: Homer's completely fabricated and inappropriately harsh 'quote'
jokes.explanation: Excellent fake quote that sounds like something Homer would believe Lombardi said. Perfectly captures his warped priorities.

jokes.text: Who wants character? Let's quit. -Okay.
jokes.setup: The pressure of competition and character building
jokes.punchline: Both kids immediately choosing to abandon the whole thing
jokes.explanation: Perfect kid wisdom. The boys cutting through all the adult nonsense with simple logic is both funny and smart.

jokes.text: this is the most stirring display... of gallantry since Mountbatten gave India back to the Punjabs.
jokes.setup: Announcer describing the boys' decision to tie
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous historical comparison to mini golf sportsmanship
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation comparing mini golf to major historical events. The specificity makes it work.

jokes.text: You're welching on our bet? -What? Neither boy lost. -But neither-- We-- You have to do it too. -Small price to see you humiliated.
jokes.setup: The tie means both fathers technically have to honor the bet
jokes.punchline: Homer's willingness to humiliate himself just to see Ned humiliated too
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer spite. His willingness to suffer just to make Ned suffer too is peak pettiness and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Small price to see you humiliated.
jokes.setup: Homer realizing he has to wear the dress too
jokes.punchline: Still considering Ned's humiliation worth his own
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - willing to endure humiliation if it means his enemy suffers too.

jokes.text: Simpson, I feel silly, but what the hay? Reminds me of my old fraternity days. -Oh, my God. He's enjoying it!
jokes.setup: Both men having to mow lawns in dresses as punishment
jokes.punchline: Ned's positive attitude ruins Homer's intended humiliation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion. Ned's ability to find joy in anything completely defeats Homer's spiteful plan. Perfect character consistency and great payoff.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. He's enjoying it!
jokes.setup: Ned being positive about wearing the dress
jokes.punchline: Homer's horror that his revenge backfired completely
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending - Homer's plan completely backfires. Ned's unshakeable positivity defeats all attempts at humiliation.
